title: AP 物理C力学 2015年国际卷

questions.prompt: 1. In which of the graphs is the magnitude of the momentum of the object increasing?
2. In which of the graphs does the object have a constant nonzero kinetic energy?
3. In which of the graphs is the net force on the object zero?

4. The coplanar forces $F_{1}, F_{2}, F_{3}$, and $F_{4}$ represented above can act on an object. If the object is initially at rest, which combination of the forces would result in static equilibrium?

5. A block of mass $M$ is being pulled to the right at a constant speed with a pull force $F_{\text {pull }}$ that is at an angle of $\theta$ with respect to the horizontal, as shown in the figure above. The force of friction acting on the block has magnitude $f$. What is the magnitude of $F_{p u l l}$ ?
6. What is the magnitude of the average force exerted by the club on the ball during contact?
7. What is the work done by the club on the ball?
8. What is the maximum height above the Moon's surface attained by the ball?
9. A simple pendulum of length $L$ has a period $T$. What is the length of another simple pendulum of period $2 T$, assuming that both pendulums swing with small amplitude?

10. Pendulum 1 is displaced by a very small angle $\theta_{1}$, is released from rest, and oscillates with a period $T_{1}$. Pendulum 2 is identical to pendulum 1 , but it is displaced by a very small angle $\theta_{2}$, is released from rest, and oscillates with a period $T_{2}$, where $\theta_{1}>\theta_{2}$. Using the small angle approximation, how do the periods $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ compare?

11. A newly discovered planet has three times the mass and five times the radius of Earth. What is the ratio of the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the new planet to the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of Earth?

12. A satellite is in an elliptical orbit about a planet, as shown in the figure above. At apogee, a distance $R_{1}$ from the planet, the satellite's angular speed is $\omega$. What is the angular speed of the satellite at perigee, a distance $R_{2}=R_{1} / 2$ from the planet?

13. Two objects of equal mass $m$ are tied to a string and placed on a horizontal frictionless tabletop. The string and objects spin with angular velocity $\omega$ so that the objects move in circles of radius $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$, as shown above. The tensions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ must satisfy which of the following relations?

14. A block of mass 3 kg is swung on a cord in a horizontal circle of radius 2 m . The speed of the block is $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The magnitude of the acceleration of the block is most nearly

15. Two 1.0 kg balls have a perfectly elastic head-on collision, as shown in the figure above. Before the collision, ball $X$ has a velocity of $2.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to the right and ball $Y$ has a velocity of $4.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to the left. After the collision, ball $X$ has a velocity of

16. An object of mass $m$ and velocity $3 v$ toward the east has a completely inelastic collision with an object of mass $2 m$ and velocity $2 v$ toward the north. After the collision, the momentum of the combined object has a magnitude of

17. A 2.0 kg block is released from rest at the top of a rough plane inclined at $37^{\circ}$ to the horizontal, as shown above. The block slides down the incline with an acceleration of $4.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. What is the approximate magnitude of the force of friction on the block as it slides down the incline?

18. A 2.0 kg box is on top of a 4.0 kg box that is placed on a frictionless surface, as shown in the figure above. A 3.0 N force is applied to the top box. What is the minimum coefficient of friction between the two boxes needed to keep them moving together?
19. What is the magnitude of the acceleration of the center of mass of the three-object system at this instant?
20. What is the velocity of the center of mass of the three-object system at this instant?

21. In a classroom demonstration, a teacher has a coin and feather in a long tube. The teacher uses a vacuum pump to remove all the air from the tube. The feather and coin are then dropped from the top of the tube at the same time. Which of the following describes what happens and why?

22. The graph above shows velocity versus time for an object moving in a straight line, initially in the $+x$ direction. Which of the following is true for the motion of the object for the entire time shown on the graph?
23. If instead of being lowered slowly to equilibrium, the block-still attached to the spring-was released from rest and allowed to fall, which of the following would happen?

24. The spring and attached block are allowed to come to rest at position $X$ and are then moved to rest at position $Y$ along the diagonal path shown above. What is the work done on the block by the spring in moving it along this path?

25. The sphere shown above, which hangs from a rigid wire, is given a twist and set into angular harmonic motion. The resulting equation of motion of the oscillating sphere is $\theta(t)=0.1 \cos (2 t)$, where $\theta$ is in radians and $t$ is in seconds. If the rotational inertia of the sphere is $10 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, then the torsion constant of the wire is most nearly
26. Assuming air resistance is negligible, how does a diver doing flips off a diving board change rotational speed in midair?

27. A toy spacecraft is launched directly upward. When the toy reaches its highest point, a spring is released and the toy splits into two parts with masses of 0.01 kg and 0.09 kg , respectively. Immediately after the separation, the 0.01 kg part moves horizontally due east. Air resistance is negligible. True statements about the 0.09 kg part include which of the following? I. It could move north immediately after the spring is released. II. It takes longer to reach the ground than does the 0.01 kg part. III. It strikes the ground closer to the launch point than does the 0.01 kg part.
28. A system consists of three 2 kg objects located at coordinates $(0,2),(0,0)$, and $(2,0)$, as shown in the figure above. What are the coordinates $(x, y)$ of the center of mass of the system?
29. The speed of the ball as it swings through point $P$ is

30. If air resistance and friction in the pivot are negligible, quantities that are conserved as the ball swings back and forth include which of the following? I. Mechanical energy of the ball-Earth system II. Linear momentum of the ball III. Angular momentum of the ball about the pivot point
31. The graph above shows potential energy $U$ versus position $x$ for an object. Which of the following graphs shows the force $F$ exerted on the object as a function of position $x$ ?

32. A block of mass $m$ on a horizontal surface is attached to one end of a horizontal spring. The other end of the spring is fixed, as shown in the figure above. The block oscillates with negligible friction and an amplitude of 4.0 cm . How far from the equilibrium position is the block when the kinetic energy of the block is three times the potential energy of the system?
33. An Earth satellite in a circular orbit of radius $R$ has a period $T$. What is the period of an Earth satellite in a circular orbit of radius $4 R$ ?

34. The net force on an object initially moving along the positive $x$-axis is given by $F(t)=\left(4 t-t^{3}\right)$ for $t>0$, where $F$ is in newtons and along the $x$-axis, and $t$ is in seconds. At what time does the object reach its maximum positive velocity?
35. A heavy sign of mass $M$ is hung between two buildings from two supporting wires, each making an angle $\theta$ with the vertical, as shown in the figure above. What is the tension in each wire?

A new sports car is undergoing acceleration tests to determine its specifications. The following data on speed $v$ versus time $t$ are recorded for the car as it accelerates from rest along a straight track.

parts.partContent: On the axes below, plot $v$ as a function of $t$ and sketch a curve that best represents the data.

Answer each of the following for the time period $t=0 \mathrm{~s}$ to $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$.
i. Does the speed of the car increase, decrease, or stay the same?
$\_\_\_\_$ Increase $\_\_\_\_$ Decrease $\_\_\_\_$ Stay the same
Justify your answer.
ii. Does the acceleration of the car increase, decrease, or stay the same?
$\_\_\_\_$ Increase $\_\_\_\_$ Decrease $\_\_\_\_$ Stay the same
Justify your answer.
Explain how you would use your graph in part

to find the distance traveled by the car between $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$.

The equation for the speed $v$ of the car as a function of time $t$ found from the graph is $v(t)=-0.3 t^{2}+7 t$, where $v$ is in meters per second and $t$ is in seconds.
Derive an expression for the acceleration of the car $a(t)$ as a function of time $t$.

Calculate the position of the car as a function of time, $x(t)$, assuming that the car starts from rest at the origin of a coordinate system.
(f) Calculate the distance traveled by the car between $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$.

questions.prompt: } The model of a roller coaster shown above consists of a ramp and a circular loop. A small block of mass $M$ starts from height $H$ with speed $v=0$ and slides without friction down the ramp and around the inside of the loop of radius $H / 2$. Position $P$ is a point on the loop at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal, as shown above. Express all algebraic answers in terms of $M, H, \theta$, and fundamental constants, as appropriate.

parts.partContent: Derive an expression for the speed of the block at position $P$.

On the following diagram, draw and label vectors to represent the forces (not components) on the block when it is at position $P$. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the dot at position $P$.
Derive an expression for the magnitude of the force exerted by the loop on the block when it is at position $P$. If you need to draw anything other than what you have shown in part
to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do NOT add anything to the figure in part
Calculate the angle $\theta_{L}$, in degrees, at which the block leaves the loop.

The small block is replaced with a small sphere that also starts at height $H$. Now there is enough friction to allow the sphere to roll down the ramp without slipping. Identify how the new angle $\theta_{n}$ at which the sphere leaves the loop compares with the angle $\theta_{L}$ calculated in part

questions.prompt: } A uniform, rigid, thin board that is 2.4 m long and weighs 200 N is attached to a post by a pivot at point $P$ and hangs over the edge of a building, as shown above. A crate weighing 400 N is attached to the right end of the board. The left end of the board, 0.80 m from the pivot, is attached to a light vertical wire anchored at the other end to keep the board from rotating.

parts.partContent: On the diagram below, draw and label vectors to represent all the forces (not components) acting on the board. Show each force vector originating at its point of application.
Calculate the magnitudes of the forces exerted on the board by the post and by the wire. If you need to draw anything other than what you have shown in part
to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do NOT add anything to the figure in part

The rotational inertia of the board alone about its center is $\frac{1}{12} M L^{2}$, where $M$ is the mass of the board and $L$ is its length. Calculate the rotational inertia of the combined board-crate system about point $P$.

Suppose that the wire breaks and the board begins to pivot about point $P$. Calculate each of the following.
i. The magnitude of the initial angular acceleration of the board-crate system
ii. The magnitude of the initial linear acceleration of the left end of the board

3 points


For accurately plotting at least five points

For a smooth curve with a positive slope
1 point
For a concave down curve starting at the origin
1 point

i. $\quad 1$ point

The correct choice is "Increase"
For a correct justification

Example: The speed increases as the magnitude of the velocity increases for each data point
ii. 1 point

The correct choice is "Decrease"
For a correct justification

Example: Since the slope of the line decreases with time, the acceleration of the car must decrease with time

2 points

For mentioning area under the curve

For specifically describing the area between $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$

Example: To determine the distance traveled from a velocity-time graph, one should find the area under the curve. For time $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ to $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, one can approximate the area using a trapezoid that approximately fills the area under the curve.

2 points

For beginning with an indication that the acceleration is the derivative of velocity
$$
a(t)=\frac{d v}{d t}
$$

For correctly evaluating the derivative

1 point
$$
a(t)=\frac{d}{d t}\left(-0.3 t^{2}+7 t\right)=-0.6 t+7
$$

3 points}

For an indication that the displacement is the integral of the velocity
$\Delta x=\int_{t_{0}}^{t} v(t) d t$

For attempting to integrate with appropriate limits or constant of integration
$\Delta x=\int_{0}^{t}\left(-0.3 t^{2}+7 t\right) d t$

For the correct answer
$x(t)=-0.1 t^{3}+3.5 t^{2}$

1 point
(f) 3 points

For recognizing that the distance is the change in position
1 point

$\Delta x=x(8)-x(2)$

For using the expression from part

to calculate $x(2)$ and $x(8)$
$x(2)=13.2 \mathrm{~m}$
$x(8)=172.8 \mathrm{~m}$

For the correct answer with units

$\Delta x=159.6 \mathrm{~m}$
Alternate solution

Alternate points
1 point

1 point

For using the integral of the speed and recognizing that the distance is the change in position
For using the correct limits or constant of integration
$\Delta x=\int_{2}^{8}\left(-0.3 t^{2}+7 t\right) d t$
$\Delta x=\frac{-0.3 t^{3}}{3}+\left.\frac{7 t^{2}}{2}\right|_{2} ^{8}=-51.2+224-(-0.8+14)$

For the correct answer with units
$\Delta x=159.6 \mathrm{~m}$
1 point

3 points

For a correct application of conservation of energy
$K_{1}+U_{1}=K_{2}+U_{2}$
$m g H=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+m g y_{P}$
For relating $y_{p}$ to $H$ and $\theta$
$y_{p}=\frac{H}{2}+\frac{H}{2} \sin \theta$
For a correct final expression for $v$, which does not have to be simplified
$v=\sqrt{g H(1-\sin \theta)}$

For correctly drawing and labeling a vector for the weight of the block

For correctly drawing and labeling a vector for the normal force on the block

One earned point is deducted (up to a maximum of two points) for each extraneous force drawn

5 points

For applying Newton's second law with more than one force

For setting the sum of the forces in the radial direction equal to $m v^{2} / R$
$F_{N}+w_{\text {radial }}=\frac{m v^{2}}{R}$
For using the correct radial component of the weight
$w_{\text {radial }}=m g \sin \theta$
For correctly substituting for the speed and radius from part

$F_{N}+m g \sin \theta=\frac{m g H(1-\sin \theta)}{H / 2}$
For an answer consistent with part
$F_{N}=m g(2-3 \sin \theta)$

2 points

For setting the normal force equal to zero (explicitly or implicitly)
$0=m g(2-3 \sin \theta)$
$3 \sin \theta=2$
$\theta=\sin ^{-1}(2 / 3)$
For an answer consistent with part

$\theta=41.8^{\circ}$

For correctly selecting $\theta_{n}<\theta_{L}$
1 point
For indicating that the sphere will be moving slower (or has less kinetic energy) when it 1 point loses contact
For indicating that some of the initial potential energy now goes into rotational kinetic 1 point energy
Example: Since some of the energy must go into the rotational energy of the sphere, the sphere will have slower translational speed at every point on the track. Since the sphere moves slower than the block, the sphere will lose contact with the loop at a lower height and a smaller angle.

3 points


For correctly drawing and labeling the three downward forces: tension, weight of the board, weight of the crate
For correctly drawing and labeling the upward force exerted on the board by the post

For drawing all of the forces at their respective points of action

One earned point is deducted (up to a maximum of two points) for each extraneous force drawn

4 points

For summing the torques about any labeled point and setting that sum equal to zero 1 point Example using left end as pivot point:
$\tau_{\mathrm{cw}}-\tau_{\mathrm{ccw}}=0$
$\tau_{w \text {-board }}+\tau_{w \text {-crate }}=\tau_{\text {post }}$
For substituting correct radii for all torques
$(200 \mathrm{~N})(1.2 \mathrm{~m})+(400 \mathrm{~N})(2.4 \mathrm{~m})=\left(F_{\text {post }}\right)(0.8 \mathrm{~m})$
For correctly calculating either the tension or the post force 1 point
$F_{\text {post }}=1500 \mathrm{~N}$
For a correct method for determining the remaining force and a consistent answer
1 point
$F_{\text {up }}=F_{\text {down }}$
$F_{\text {post }}=w_{\text {board }}+w_{\text {crate }}+F_{T}$
$F_{T}=1500 \mathrm{~N}-200 \mathrm{~N}-400 \mathrm{~N}$
$F_{T}=900 \mathrm{~N}$

3 points

For correctly calculating the rotational inertia of the board about $P$
$I_{\text {board }-P}=M d^{2}+I_{\mathrm{cm}}=M d^{2}+\frac{1}{12} M L^{2}$
$I_{\text {board }-P}=\left(\frac{200 \mathrm{~N}}{9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}}\right)(0.4 \mathrm{~m})^{2}+\frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{200 \mathrm{~N}}{9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}}\right)(2.4 \mathrm{~m})^{2}=13.1 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
$$
\left(\text { or } 12.8 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2} \text { using } g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)
$$

For correctly calculating the rotational inertia of the crate about $P$
$I=m_{\text {crate }} r^{2}=\left(\frac{400 \mathrm{~N}}{9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}}\right)(1.6)^{2}=104.5 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
$$
\left(\text { or } 102.4 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2} \text { using } g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)
$$

For recognizing that the total rotational inertia of the system about $P$ is the sum of the 1 point inertia of the board and the crate about $P$
$\Sigma I_{P}=117.6 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ (or $115.2 \mathrm{~kg} \cdot \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ using $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )

i. $\quad 2$ points

For summing torques about $P$, setting the sum equal to $I \alpha$, and substituting appropriate values
$\Sigma \tau_{P}=w_{\text {board }} d_{\text {board }}+w_{\text {crate }} d_{\text {crate }}=I \alpha$
$(200 \mathrm{~N})(0.4 \mathrm{~m})+(400 \mathrm{~N})(1.6 \mathrm{~m})=115.2 \alpha$
For an answer consistent with part

$\alpha=6.13 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \quad\left(\right.$ or $6.25 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ using $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
ii. $\quad 2$ points

For a correct relationship between angular and linear acceleration
$a=r \alpha$
$a=(0.80 \mathrm{~m})\left(6.13 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
For an answer consistent with part
